questions.text: I rarely notice shifts in someone's vocal tone during a conversation.
I prefer clearly defined roles and expectations in collaborative work.
I sense the social temperature of an email based on word choice, punctuation, and formatting.
I tend to put other people's needs ahead of my own, even when it costs me.
I tend to experience emotions as informational signals rather than as overwhelming forces.
I have been praised for my ability to stay rational when emotions run high.
I tend to wait until the pressure of a deadline sharpens my focus.
I experience boredom not as a quiet absence but as an agitating restlessness.
I feel that the effort of maintaining strict order often outweighs the benefits.
I tend to view emotional intensity in others with a kind of curiosity because my own experience is different.
When there is a vacuum of leadership, I look around to see who else might step in.
I find that I am unapologetically focused on building the life I envision for myself.
I believe that the obligation to respond to suffering is the moral truth I hold most firmly, regardless of how inconvenient acting on it may be.
I believe that strong leadership requires the willingness to override dissent when needed.
I find that maintaining emotional equilibrium during painful situations allows me to focus on what needs to happen rather than on how the situation makes me feel.
I am happy to let someone else be the spokesperson.
I hold the view that every person's first responsibility is to themselves.
I hold the view that explicit communication is a kindness, not a lack of sophistication.
